hook: Two licences. One placement. Most composers only know about one.

slides.headline: Two licences. One placement. Most composers only know about one.
If you've ever wondered why sync deals feel complicated — every placement requires two separate legal permissions.

🔑 THE SYNC LICENCE
Permission to pair your composition with visual content.
slides.lede: Controlled by: the publisher (or you, if you self-publish).

slides.headline:
🔑 THE MASTER LICENCE
Permission to use the specific recording.
slides.lede: Controlled by: whoever owns the master — often the label, unless you recorded independently. · One placement = two separate negotiations.

slides.headline: Here's what you need to know:
slides.lede: If a label owns your master: · They share in every sync deal you ever make · A supervisor has to negotiate with two parties · That friction kills deals
list_items: If a label owns your master:
They share in every sync deal you ever make
A supervisor has to negotiate with two parties

slides.headline:
Supervisors prioritise one-stop tracks.
Fewer calls. Faster deals. No surprises.
Here's what you need to know:
slides.lede: What the sync licence actually covers · What the master licence covers and who typically holds it · Why one-stop status changes everything · How to protect your position before you sign anything
